meta.title: Northwind Outfitters: The E-commerce Crossroads
meta.synopsis: You are Nadia Okonkwo, CEO of Northwind Outfitters, a 40-year-old regional outdoor-gear retailer with twelve stores. Customers keep asking to buy online, two rivals just launched e-commerce, and the board wants a decision. Gather the facts from your leadership team, then choose whether — and how — to enter the online channel.
meta.source_ref: Original teaching case authored for Case Quest fixtures (fictional company).

learning_objectives.text: Understand the unit economics of a new sales channel — margins after platform fees, customer acquisition cost, and fulfillment cost — before committing to it.
Recognize operational capability gaps (fulfillment, systems, staffing) that must be closed before a new channel can succeed.
Weigh the value of a phased or pilot entry against a full commitment when demand is real but capabilities and economics are uncertain.

actors.name: Nadia Okonkwo

persona.background: CEO of Northwind Outfitters for six years; grew the store base but has never run a digital channel.
persona.personality: Decisive and customer-obsessed, but aware of what she doesn't know about e-commerce.
persona.communication_style: Asks pointed questions and expects specifics.

goals: Protect the brand and the stores
Meet customers where they now shop
Not bet the company on an unproven channel

actors.name: Marcus Reilly

persona.background: CFO who has run Northwind's finances for a decade and guards the balance sheet.
persona.communication_style: Speaks in margins, payback periods, and downside cases.

goals: Insist any new channel earns its capital

dialogue.greeting: Nadia. Before anyone gets excited about a website, let's talk about what a dollar of online revenue actually keeps.

topics.line: After marketplace fees, payment processing, and returns, online gross margin lands around 8 percent. In the stores we clear 35.

actors.name: Priya Anand

persona.background: Head of marketing; closest to the customer research and the demand signals.
persona.personality: Optimistic and data-driven, pushes for growth.
persona.communication_style: Leads with customer voice and survey numbers.

goals: Capture online demand before rivals lock it in

dialogue.greeting: The customers are already voting with their questions — let me show you the demand data.

topics.line: Forty percent of our loyalty members asked for online ordering this year. The demand is real and it's growing.

actors.name: Sam Delgado

persona.background: COO responsible for the single distribution warehouse and store logistics.
persona.personality: Grounded, protective of operations, honest about limits.
persona.communication_style: Concrete about what the operation can and cannot do today.

goals: Not promise fulfillment the operation can't deliver

actors.name: Eleanor Voss

persona.background: Board chair and former retail executive who has watched competitors move online.
persona.personality: Strategic, patient, asks about the long game.
persona.communication_style: Frames choices against the competitive landscape.

goals: Position Northwind to win over five years, not one quarter

facts.label: Online margins
facts.content: After platform fees, payment processing, and returns, e-commerce gross margin is ~8% versus ~35% in the stores.

facts.label: Available capital
facts.content: Northwind can fund a modest pilot comfortably, but a full own-platform build plus warehouse automation would strain the balance sheet.

facts.label: Customer demand
facts.content: 40% of loyalty members requested online ordering this year; demand is real and rising.

facts.label: Acquisition cost
facts.content: Paid online customer acquisition cost is high; at current margins, payback is roughly 14 months per new online customer.

facts.label: Fulfillment gap
facts.content: The warehouse is built for store replenishment, not direct-to-consumer picking, packing, and returns; it needs new systems and staff.

facts.label: Competitor moves
facts.content: Two rivals launched online last year: one is struggling with fulfillment, the other is growing steadily by starting narrow.

facts.label: 3PL option
facts.content: A third-party logistics provider could handle direct-to-consumer fulfillment for a pilot without Northwind building its own capability first.

facts.label: Full-launch burn
facts.content: A full own-platform launch would run a significant monthly cash burn for at least a year before contribution turns positive.

decisions.prompt: How should Northwind respond to the online opportunity?

options.label: Launch a limited pilot
options.consequence_text: You choose to test the channel before committing, keeping capital and operational risk contained.

options.label: Commit to a full e-commerce launch
options.consequence_text: You decide to go big to match rivals, accepting the capital and operational stretch.

options.label: Stay stores-only for now
options.consequence_text: You hold off entirely, betting the stores remain enough while you watch how rivals fare.

decisions.prompt: How should the pilot be run?

options.label: Sell via an existing marketplace using a 3PL
options.consequence_text: You start narrow: list a curated range on an established marketplace and outsource fulfillment, learning fast with little capital.

options.label: Build your own storefront for the pilot
options.consequence_text: You build a bespoke site for even a small pilot, sinking time and cash into capability you can't yet judge you need.

decisions.prompt: How aggressively should the full launch be funded?

options.label: Fund a measured, staged rollout
options.consequence_text: Even at full commitment you stage the build, closing capability gaps before scaling spend.

options.label: Fund an aggressive all-in build
options.consequence_text: You spend hard on platform and automation up front, racing rivals before the economics are proven.

endings.title: Holding the Line
endings.summary: Northwind stays stores-only. It stays profitable for now, but online-native rivals keep capturing the customers who wanted to buy digitally.
endings.real_case_comparison: Retailers who indefinitely deferred a channel their customers were already demanding tended to cede the segment; the demand did not wait.

lo_outcomes.verdict: You avoided a low-margin channel's risks, but the debrief shows demand this strong rarely rewards waiting.
Declining entirely forwent the low-risk option of learning through a small pilot.

endings.title: Learning Before Betting
endings.summary: By starting narrow and closing capability gaps before scaling, Northwind builds a channel that pays back — expanding only once the economics are proven.
endings.real_case_comparison: The rival that started narrow and staged its expansion is the one that grew steadily; disciplined phasing beat racing to scale.

lo_outcomes.verdict: You matched commitment to what you'd actually learned — the phased path this case rewards.
You let the unit economics prove out before scaling spend.

endings.title: Building Ahead of the Evidence
endings.summary: Northwind sinks capital into platform and fulfillment capability before demand and economics justify it. Cash burns faster than the channel matures.
endings.real_case_comparison: The rival that overbuilt fulfillment before proving demand struggled under the cost — capability outran the evidence for it.

lo_outcomes.verdict: You built capability the channel might need someday before confirming it needed it now — capability must follow proven demand, not precede it.
